nodes.question: What is the LTV/CAC ratio for this business?
What is the CAC Payback Period in months?

nodes.rationale: LTV/CAC below 1.0 means you spend more acquiring customers than they'll ever return. This is a business-model-level failure, not a marketing optimization problem. Freeze paid acquisition. Reassess pricing, churn, and product-market fit before spending another euro.
actions: Calculate honest blended vs paid CAC

nodes.rationale: LTV/CAC above 5 with slow growth indicates over-optimization. You have healthy margins but you're leaving growth on the table. Competitors with worse unit economics but faster growth will capture the market. Increase acquisition spend strategically.

LTV/CAC is healthy and Payback Period is under 12 months. This is fundraising-ready economics. Focus on scaling acquisition channels while maintaining efficiency. Monitor segmented CAC to ensure no channel is masking inefficiency.
actions: Implement segmented CAC tracking per channel/country
Monitor for CAC inflation as you scale

nodes.rationale: Payback Period between 12-18 months is tolerable for early-stage or enterprise SaaS, but requires active optimization. Focus on improving gross margin (reduce infrastructure costs), reducing sales cycle length, and increasing ARPU through upsells.
actions: Optimize cloud costs to improve gross margin
Reduce sales cycle with better qualification
Track paid-only CAC separately from blended

nodes.rationale: 18+ month payback burns cash dangerously. If churn is above 5% monthly, you may never recover CAC on many customers. Shift from high-CAC enterprise sales to product-led growth. Consider whether your pricing is too low for your sales motion.
actions: Audit full CAC including hidden costs (salaries, commissions)
Evaluate product-led growth to reduce sales-assisted CAC
Increase pricing if value justifies it

nodes.rationale: 24+ month payback with limited cash runway is existential. You are burning money on customers who may churn before ever becoming profitable. This is not a marketing problem — this is a business model problem requiring immediate CEO/Board intervention.
actions: Immediate freeze on all paid acquisition
Renegotiate all vendor contracts to cut COGS
Present honest unit economics to board/investors
